PREFACE TO SECOND EDITION 

OF 
THE BUDDHISM OF TIBET 

ITTLE positive was known about the Buddhism of Tibet 
—that far-off vast isolated towering tableland and 
“Forbidden Land” on the Roof of the World in Mid-Asia, 
and separated by the icy walls of the Himalays from the 
Indian plains, the homeland of the Buddha and his Buddhism 
—hefore the publication of my book under that title in 1895. 
That work was written under uniquely favourable circum- 
stances for penetrating for the first time the frigid reserve and 
jealously guarded mysteries of the Tibetan Buddhist priests 
or ‘““Lamas.” This Tibetan term “Lama” means in Tibetan 
literally “‘the Superior or Exalted One,” and significantly 
it rs the literal Tibetan translation of “‘ Arya” or “ Aryan,’ (from 
the Early Aryan or Sumerian Ar “exalted or noble”); but 
used in an ecclesiastic Buddhistic, and not in a racial sense. 
For, Buddha Gotama and his Buddhist ethics were purely of 
Aryan origin; and the Aryan Race, to which the purer Britons 
belong, were, as I have fully demonstrated, the originators 
and the chief propagators of the world’s civilisation, including 
the higher religions (see my Makers of Civilization in Race and 
History, London, 1929). And Buddha himself calls his 
perfected saint an Arya, Ariye and “Aryan.” 
It was after this Tibetan priestly title of ‘“‘Lama”’ (or 
‘“Aryan’’) that the Buddhism of Tibet has been called by 
Europeans ““Lamaism.” ‘This usage of that term “ Lamaism”’ 

vill PREFACE TO SECOND EDITION. 

is thus in series with “Brahman-ism,” the European title for 
the religion of the Indo-Aryan priests or Brahmans, of whose 
form of Aryan Religion and Mythology Buddhism was an 
offshoot, and at the same time, like that of its predecessor 
and kindred religion Jain-ism, a revolt against its Vedic idea 
of a Supreme Soul and its later cruel and wholesale blood- 
sacrifices with wanton destruction of animal life. Whereas, 
the title ‘‘Buddhism”’ is derived, as we shall see, from the 
religious title of its founder Gotama, namely “‘Buddha” or 
“The Enlightened One’’; and it is thus in series with the title 
‘Christianity,’ coined from the “Christ” title of Jesus; and 
therefore is unlike the title of the other great world religion 
‘“Mohammedanism”’ derived from the personal name of its 
prophet; and that of “Judaism” coined more or less directly 
from the racial term of “Jew” for that non-Aryan race. 

Brahmanism, the parent of Buddhism, as I have fully demon- 
strated, was a relatively late Indian priestly caste development 
of the purer Solar and Nature religion of the Sumerians or 
Early Aryans, imported by the latter from Babylonia into 
India in their civilization of that land about 2750 B.c. (see 
my Makers of Ciwilization, Indo-Sumerian Seals Deciphered, 
1925, Sumer-Aryan Dictionary, 1927, Aryan-Sumerian Origin 
of the Alphabet, 1927, etc., etc.). 

My SpEcIAL SourcES oF First-HAND INFORMATION. 

The previous attempts at the systematic exploration of the 
‘subject of Lamaism had been made by writers who had not 
themselves been in personal contact with Tibet and Tibetan 
Lamas. They were mere compilers at second hand of miscel- 
laneous notes and tales by ,travellers, who themselves had 
visited mostly mere outlying provincés of “The Closed Land,”’ 
and were unpossessed of any knowledge or competent know- 
ledge of the history and developments of Buddhism in and 
outside its original home in India. 

PREFACKL T'0 SECOND EDITION. i ix 

A te RS 

On the other hand, my ae ioe of Tibetan Buddhism 
and Tibetan History and Tradition, Folk-lore and Art were 
undertaken in direct contact with Central Tibet and Tibetans, 
and not until after many years previous special explorations 
and excavations at first hand of the Buddhist Holy Land in 
India; and after having previously explored at first hand the 
varied forms of Buddhism in Burma and Ceylon, on and 
Bhotan, and China and Japan. 

My Discovery oF BuppHaA’s BIRTHPLACE, ETC. 

My attention was first irresistibly attracted to Buddhism, 
as one of the great living religions of the world, by its picturesque 
temples and monks with their marvellous art, during a visit 
to Ceylon and Burma in 1879. And subsequent prolonged 
visits there enhanced the attraction and led me to study 
seriously the tenets, history and art of Buddhism. When 
later on, in the Indian Medical Service, I was stationed officially 
within the Buddhist Holy Land in India, the home of Buddha 
and his Buddhism, I discovered and excavated several of the 
lost hermitages of the historical Buddha or “‘The Enlightened 
One,” the Aryan ex-prince Gotama, “‘The Sage of the Sakya 
(or Scythic) Tribe (Sakya-Muunt), recovering teeming inscrip- 
tional and statuary stone remains, which I gifted to the 
_ India Museum of Calcutta; and recovering actual bodily relics 
of that sage; as well as eventually discovering the long lost 
birth-place of the Buddha at Kapilavastu in the Nepal Terai, 
which placed the truly historical character of the Buddha for 
the first time beyond all further doubt. 

My TIBETAN RESEARCHES AND DISCOVERIES. 

When thus equipped, I chanced to be located officially with 
my headquarters on the frontier of Central Tibet, at the 
Himalayan station of Darjeeling or properly in Tibetan 
‘‘Dorje-ling” or “The Place of the Dorje.”’ The Dorje is the 

x PREFACE TO SECOND E#HDITION. 

mystic Thunderbolt, borrowed from the Indian Jupiter or 
Indra, by which Buddha is credited with having shattered 
the demons infesting the world of man; and which is freely 
sculptured on the throne-cushion on which Buddha is repre- 
sented seated in many of the ancient Indian sculptures at 
Buddh-Gaya, where he attained Buddhahood; and where the 
traditional spot where he sat in “‘meditation”’ under the sacred 
Vig-tree (Bodhi-tree), and now enclosed within a magnificent 
ancient Buddhist temple there, is still called ‘““The Thunderbolt 
Throne.” This thunderbolt or Dorje is also figured on images 
of Buddha from other sacred Indian Buddhist sites, and 
as held in Buddha’s hand like Jupiter; and it is especially 
prominent in Tibetan Buddhism, as shown in these pages. 

At the spot where this mystic thunderbolt was reputed by 
the Lamas to have been found at Darjeeling, and giving that 
town its name, the natives, who were Lamaists and largely 
Tibetans, had erected a Lamaist temple, in which the chief 
celebrants were Tibetans. This temple was furnished with 
complete sets of the voluminous Lamaist scriptures; and 
several other and larger Lama temples with monasteries or 
Lamaderies, with still larger Tibetan libraries existed in the 
adjoining semi-independent states of Sikhim and Bhotan 
(or “The End of Bhot” ie. “Tibet’’), which were freely 
accessible to European sight-seers. Moreover, the native 
bazaar at Darjeeling, which stands on the direct trade route 
from India to Lhasa (the Tibetan capital, and most sacred 
city of the Lamas, the seat of their incarnate Buddha-god, 
the Dalai Lama), was frequented by crowds of picturesque 
Tibetan merchants with their wives and families, many of 
whom had with their caravans left Lhasa only some ten or 
twelve days previously. And more especially, along with 
these were learned Lamas from Lh4sa and other Tibetan centres, 
and even from far-off Mongolia who were going to, or returning 
from pilgrimages to the ancient Buddhist shrines in India. 

PREFACE TO SECOND EDITION. xi 

Seizing advantage of this unique opportunity for exploring 
the then comparatively little known Lamaism, I took up the 
intensive study of the Tibetan language and literature in order 
to converse freely with the Tibetans and their Lamas, and 
read their scriptures. I also employed Tibetans as my personal 
servants, and engaged two very learned Lamas from the 
Dalai Lama’s own monastery at Lhasa, one a Tibetan and the 
other a Mongol (who had been induced by the Indian Govern- 
ment to settle at Darjeeling to teach literary Tibetan to selected 
young semi-Tibetan lads there to travel in Tibet as spies, 
mapping it out for our Survey Department) as tutors and 
searchers of the voluminous Tibetan Buddhist texts which 
they were procuring me. The Tibetan language proved all the 
easier, as it belonged to the same linguistic Mongolian family 
as the Burmese with which I was fairly familiar, having just 
returned from over a year’s active campaigning military 
medical service with our expedition which had annexed 
Upper Burma to the borders of Tibet, on the dethronement of 
Theebaw, the last of the Burmese kings. Moreover, the 
Tibetan script is written in that form of the Indian Sanskrit 
writing which was current in India from the seventh to the 
tenth centuries A.D., the epoch when Tibet obtained its 
Buddhism and bulky Buddhist scriptures from India, at a 
time when Buddhism was still the dominant religion in India. 
And it had been one of my first self-imposed tasks on entering 
the Indian Medical Service in Bengal to pass the Higher 
Standard literary examinations in, not only the universal 
Indian vernacular Hindustani, but also in Bengali and in 
Sanskrit, the dead literary language of Indian Buddhism and 
Brahmanism, corresponding to the Latin of Europe. 

In order the more effectually to penetrate the jealous reserve 
of the Lamas and gain an intimate knowledge of their mystic 
Buddhist ritual and beliefs, I purchased the complete fittings 
of a Lamaist temple: altars, images, and painted scrolls of the 

call PREFACE TO SECOND EDITION. 

chief Buddhist deities, sacrificial vessels and utensils, drums, 
trumpets, masks for the sacred plays, robes and the chief 
Lamaist scriptures from Lhasa and elsewhere in Tibet; and 
erected within my premises at Darjeeling a Lamaist temple 
with its usual carvings, exterior and interior. And I prevailed 
on the officiating Lamas to impart to me their hidden mystic 
rites. This they did freely, in the belief, as they naively 
informed me, that I must have been a Buddhist in my “previous 
‘irth,” or even a reflex of the ‘‘ Buddha-god of the West,’ as 
otherwise I could not have known so much about Buddha 
and Buddhism. And besides visiting all the Lama temples 
in Sikhim and some of those in Bhotan, I contrived on three 
occasions to evade the Tibetan frontier guards and penetrate 
some distance into “The Closed Land”’ itself. Moreover, 
with the aid of especially scholarly Lamas from Lhasa, I 
compiled a Sanskrit-Tibetan Dictionary, much more complete 
than any hitherto extant. 

As a result, so intricate and exhaustive were the results of 
my new explorations into The Buddhism of Tibet or Lamaism, 
published under that title in 1895, that when ten years later the 
Younghusband military expedition or ‘Lhasa Mission” (of 
which I was principal medical officer and official collector of 
Buddhist books and curios for the British and Indian museums) 
penetrated by force of arms from India through The Forbidden 
Land and captured and occupied the Dalai Lama’s capital 
at Lhasa in Central Tibet, comparatively little fresh information 
was elicited then or subsequently, beyond the acquisition of 
new photographs of the shrines and monasteries and monks, 
with several complete sets of the bulky Buddhist scriptures 
and their Commentaries which I procured for the British 
Museum and India Office libraries, but the contents of the 
chief of which had already been sifted in my book. 

As that book has never been superseded, and has long been 
out of print, but is still in demand, the present reprint is 

PREFACE TO SECOND EDITION. xill 

issued by the photographic process, as the blocks of the 
numerous and largely original illustrations were accidentally 
destroyed. And in the present somewhat lengthy new Preface 
is summarized the chief additional information gained at 
Lhasa, of which further details will be found along with very 
numerous new illustrations from my own drawings and photo- 
graphs in my Lhasa and the Mysteries. And some outstanding © 
points regarding the origin of Tibetan Buddhism in relation 
to Indian Buddhism that required reinforcing are now further 
emphasised ; and the printer’s errata are corrected in the text. 

TIBETAN BUDDHISM OF INDIAN ORIGIN AND TYPE. 

Contrary to current general belief amongst Europeans, 
fostered by uninformed writers on Buddhism, the orthodox 
Buddhism in Tibet or Lamaism is not a radical transformation 
of Indian Buddhism by Tibetans. It is essentially and in 
its details of the very same type as the popular later theistic 
or Maha-ydna (or “Great Vehicle”) form of Buddhism which 
had early developed in India, and which was predominantly 
current there between the seventh and twelfth centuries A.D., 
at the period when Buddhism was transplanted from India to 
Tibet by Indian Buddhist Aryan monks, under the active 
patronage of Tibetan kings, who had become proselytized to 
Buddhism as recorded in my book. 

Buddhism, as we shall see, never became a truly popular 
religion even in India until it was later developed more or 
less on theistic lines. And it was in its advanced theistic 
popular form of Maha-y4ana that it was transplanted from India, 
not only to Tibet, but also to Mongolia and China and thence 
to Japan. 

The differences between orthodox Tibetan Buddhism and 
that of the dominant form of Buddhism evolved and prevalent 
in India, before the final extinction of Buddhism in India by 
the ruthless wholesale massacre of its helpless monks by the 

XiV PREFACE TO SECOND EDITION. 

fanatical iconoclastic Mohammadan invaders in the twelfth 
century A.D., are seen to be relatively slight and immaterial. 
These are seen to consist chiefly in the inclusion of some 
indigenous demons in addition to the Indian, and _ slight 
adaptation of the Rebirth theory due to the usurpation by 
the Lamaist pope of the temporal rule of the land, which has 
made Tibet the most priest-ridden land on earth. 

Incidentally, it is noteworthy that it was this introduction 
of Indian Buddhism of Aryan Origin by Indo-Aryan monks into 
Tibet that first brought the beginnings of Civilization with 
writing to the previously barbaric and illiterate Tibetan 
mongoloids; and thus confirming still further the Aryan 
origin of the world’s civilization. 

In order to understand aright how Gotama Buddha’s own 
Buddhism had to become radically altered by its Indian 
monkish priests in a theistic direction, before it became in 
any sense a popular working religion even in India, the home 
of its birth, and before it became transplanted thence to Burma 
and Ceylon and latterly to Tibet as well as China, Mongolia, 
and Japan, it is necessary for us to glance with clear thinking 
at the real essentials of Gotama Buddha’s own Buddhism, as 
elicited by my discoveries, and stripped from the fictions, 
exaggerations and canting jargon in which most modern 
European visionary writers have invested it. 

THe TrutH.ABoUT BuppHA’s Own BUDDHISM AND ITs 
RapIcAL DEFECTS AS A PoPpuLAR RELIGION. 

Buddha’s own “Buddhism” was essentially a doctrine of 
sheer negation and despair. It was not even, nor purported 
to be, in itself a Religion, or a religion for the many, though 
incorporating some age-old mild ethics in its Law of Piety. 

During Gotama’s lifetime between the fourth and fifth 
centuries B.C., and for about a century thereafter, it was merely 

PREFACE TO SECOND EDITION. XV 

one of the many small mendicant ascetic sects arising within 
Brahmanism, and engaged expressly in each member selfishly 
seeking his own individual salvation, through his own sectarial 
notion of attaining it. And, like the others, it lacked all the 
elements of a practical popular working religion for normal 
and healthy minded and progressive work-a-day people of the 
outside populace at large, on whose charity Gotama and his 
mendicant monk followers nevertheless lived and depended. 

Gotama’s inveterately fatalistic outlook on life (possible 
mostly in the orient to which even the developed forms of 
Buddhism were confined) is traditionally ascribed to his shock 
at seeing a dead body, and having about the same time lost 
his father’s petty kingdom. 

In his despair, after dreamily sitting for some months 
cross-legged, in the Indian fashion, in shut-eyed introspective 
‘*Meditation,’ he believed he had found a new “‘Truth’’ or 
new ‘‘Revelation’’; and he arose and called himself ‘“‘The 
Buddha” or “The Enlightened One.” He proclaimed his 
morose nihilistic doctrine: that there is no Absolute or Perma- 
nent Being, so that all Life in any form, and even in the heavens 
of the Brahmanist gods (in which he believed), was not worth 
living. That, according to his basal dogma (which is still 
mechanically repeated endlessly by the more primitive Budd- 
hists on their rosary beads): “All Life (even of the gods) is 
Transitory, Miserable, and Unreal (Illusory).’’ And that man’s 
sole Salvation was, the cowardly and selfish one, for each 
individual man (for Gotama placed woman so low as to withhold 
salvation from her until in “‘a future birth” she should be so 
fortunate as to be reborn as a man) strenuously to try to 
escape finally and completely outside all Life and Existence 
in any form, with its supposed endless cycles of rebirths after 
death for the same individual soul, into the haven of Total 
Extinction of all existence in Nirvana (literally ‘blown out” 
like an extinguished candle-flame). 

Xvi PREFACE TO SECOND EDITION. 

Escape from life and its cycle of rebirths, according to 
Buddha, borrowing from the Brahmanism of his time, could 
not be effected by committing suicide. For that would 
merely plunge him deeper for ages into Hell, whence he would 
eventually emerge, if he had acquired sufficient merit there, 
as a lower animal; and in the further cycle of rebirth might 
later be again reborn as a man, no better off than before. 

Buddha’s “Way” to Salvation had very little novel in it, 
different from that of the Brahmanism of his day, and more 
especially from that of Jainism, which preceded him, and 
whose doctrine Buddha largely accepted, and borrowed its 
terms of “‘Nirvana’’ etc. Indeed, Buddha’s Buddhism is 
really an offshoot from Jainism, which still survives in India 
to this day; and which through the Brahmanist theory of 
rebirths opposed the Brahmanist sacrifice of all animal life, 
even for food; and called their perfected saint who over- 
came the cycle of rebirths and reached Nirvana by the name 
of Jina or “The Victor,” a term whence that religion gets its 
name; and for which Gotama substituted ‘“‘ Buddha” or 
“The Enlightened.” 

Buddha’s system and terminology is thus mainly borrowed 
from and based upon that of Brahmanism and Jainism. Thus 
his term Karma in Retribution for former and present Acts or 
Deeds (an Indian term now familiar to Europeans through 
the ‘Theosophists) on which the well-being or suffering of the 
individual soul is held to depend, was already an age-old. 
Aryan term and doctrine in Brahmanism and used by the 
Jains. It finds expression in Job (a book declared by biblical 
critics to be of Aryan and non-Jewish origin) as “‘They that 
sow wickedness reap the same (48); and repeated by St. Paul 
(educated in the Aryan schools of Tarsus) as ‘ Whatsoever 
a man soweth that shall he reap”’ (Galatians vi. 7). 

Similarly his advocacy of Piety and the doing of good deeds 
in order to obtain “Merit” as good marks for the individual 

PREFACE TO SECOND EDITION. XV 

in his next rebirth were old tenets in Brahmanism. In his 
mild ethics, brotherly love was urged more than in contempor- 
ary Brahmanism. Therules against the taking of life were even 
more strict in Jainism than in Buddhism. The Indian term of 
Dharma was selected by Buddha as the designation of his 
sacred doctrine or ‘‘Law.’’ And it was possibly because the 
deified Dharma was personified by the Brahmans as the god 
of Law and Justice, and the Judge of the Dead that Buddha 
made his personified Dharma or Law one of the members of 
his Buddhist triad, namely: Buddha, Dharma (or Buddha’s 
Law), Sangha (or the assembly of his monks), forcing the 
Buddhist Church, and from which the laity were excluded. 
The chief and almost sole essential difference of Buddha’s 
Buddhism from Jainism was in his Nirvana being total ex- 
tinction of the soul, whilst the Jain Nirvana was total extinction 
of the individuality of the soul by mergence into the Universal 
Soul. 

Even the first step in “The Way ”—‘“the Pilgrim’s Way ’”’— 
in Buddha’s own Buddhism was utterly impracticable and 
unnatural as a popular working religion, apart from its 
depreciation of woman. He demanded in his Renunciation of 
Life, as the first step that every man should cut himself off 
from all family and worldly ties, by abandoning home and 
worldly goods, and deserting one’s wife and child, as Gotama 
himself had done, and became a homeless wandering mendicant, 
living idly, “‘in meditation” on the Miseries of Life, upon the 
charity of the working laymen. 

Such a comical notion of one half of the world living idly and 
parasitically on the work-a-day other half, might possibly 
have occurred to a loose-thinking Indian accustomed to see 
the swarms of beggars infesting Indian bazaars and towns, and 
more especially the sacred Brahmanist city of Benares, where 
Buddha first proclaimed his doctrine—though such a notion, 
but not from any religious motive, seems now to be gaining 

xvi PREFACE TO SECOND EDITION. 

ground nearer home, in the modern aspirations of the com- 
munist and socialist unemployed and unemployable. 

But, besides its unpracticability as a popular religion (apart 
from the Aryan ethics engrafted on it), and the gross injustice 
inflicted on the work-a-day populace, its utter selfishness was 
betrayed by Buddha’s confession that it was only the one very 
rare and quite exceptionally lucky individual who attained 
Buddhahood (or the perfect saintship of Arya) in this life, 
who could hope for Nirvana after death. All that even the 
mendicant Buddhist monks could hope for was that in some 
more or less remote future birth they might eventually attain 
Nirvana, provided they accumulated sufficient Merit or good 
marks; but they always were faced by the ever-present 
possibility of losing their merit and being reborn as a lower 
animal. Whilst the laity, who though supporting the mendi- 
cant monks, did not themselves become homeless mendicant 
monks, had practically no chance at all. 

The logical outcome of such notions, such as for example, 
the question ““How would the hordes of Buddhist mendicant 
monks be fed in the event of all the human race becoming 
monks?’ never occurred to Buddha and his monks. Nor was 
any attempted explanation offered for the other hundred and 
one outstanding objections to his “Way of Salvation” based 
upon such utterly false conceptions of Animal Life and Man’s 
higher Destiny. Nor was even a recording agency or angel 
for registering man’s merit and misdeeds provided. 

Those whimsical doctrines of a shut-eyed dreamy introspec- 
tive and pessimistic Indian sophist upon human and animal 
life and his ‘“Way” of desired escape therefrom, attracted 
few followers in Buddha’s day. His doctrine would doubtless 
have died a natural death, soon after the demise of its promul- 
gator, like the hundreds of similar fantastic notions which 
were, and still to the present day are being, evolved and 
proclaimed by homeless Hindu ascetics, also sitting in the 

PREFACE TO SECOND EDITION. xix 

same Brahmanist cross-legged posture and at the same identical 
holy place, Benares, the old Brahmanist city of temples on 
the Ganges. 

ASOKA, THE CONSTANTINE OF BUDDHISM, TRANSFORMS 
BUDDHA’S DOCTRINE INTO A RELIGION c. 261 B.C. 

That Buddha’s doctrine did survive, though in somewhat 
modified form, was mainly, if not entirely, owing to the great 
Indo-Aryan emperor Asoka, who adopted it as the state religion 
of his vast empire about a century after the death of Gotama 
_ Buddha—which event, according to the latest estimates of 
Kern and Westerguard occurred between 388 and 370 B.c. 
But in order to attract the people, Buddha’s doctrine had to 
undergo considerable alterations in Asoka’s hands. 

Asoka has been well called ‘‘The Constantine of Buddhism,”’ 
a religion which has many parallels with Christianity in its 
creed, church and propaganda. He was the greatest and 
mightiest of all emperors of India. His enormous empire 
extending from sea to sea exceeded the extent of British India 
by including Afghanistan and Swat, Kashmir and Nepal. 
_ He inherited this vast empire from his grandfather, the famous 
Indo-Aryan warrior-emperor Chandra-gupta, the Sandra- 
cottus of the Greek historians, who successfully opposed 
Alexander-the-Great in his ill-fated attempt to annex India, 
shortly before his premature death in Babylon in 323 B.c. 
Chandra-gupta drove Alexander’s generals out of India and 
the Indus Valley and Afghanistan, making himself master of 
that vast empire. Here incidentally, I may mention that in 
following the tracks of Alexander and Sandracottus and Asoka 
in the Indus Valley and North-west Indian and Afghanistan 
frontiers, where Asoka’s monuments still exist and one of his 
great stupa-mounds in the Swat Valley of Afghanistan was 
excavated by me with the recovery of Buddha’s bodily relics, 
I repeatedly visited the great mound erected by Alexancer on 

XX PREFACE TO SECOND EDITION. 

the spot where his favourite charger Bucephalus was killed 
under him in forcing the passage of the Hydaspes, the modern 
Jhelum river (headwater of the Indus, where it issues from 
the Kashmir mountains) against the army of the Indian King 
Porus, an ally of Sandracottus. Here also, as indicating the 
prime importance of the Indus River to India, it is noteworthy 
that it was this greatest and longest of all the riverways of 
India, arising in the old gold-fields of Tibet, that gave its 
name as “India” to that land, and to its people as “‘ Indians”’; 
and that later aspirated gave the people their title of ““ Hindoo”’ 
and to their land ‘“‘Hindo-stan”’ or Land of the Hindoos. 

ASOKA’'S POLITICO-UTOPIAN ADOPTION OF BUDDHISM. 

Asoka was not a Buddhist in the early part of his reign. 
His reign was so peaceful, except for his conquest of Kalinga 
in Southern India, that he conferred on his great empire of 
divers races and creeds a peace, comparable only to the later 
Pax Romana or Britannica. And it seems to me not im- 
probable that Asoka adopted Buddha’s mild ethics of brotherly 
love and piety and its non-caste system, not merely in remorse 
at the enormous loss of life in his Kalinga conquest, but mainly 
as the political means of welding together the motley peoples 
and faiths within his vast empire, in an attempt to realize a 
Golden Age on earth. For Asoka everywhere is disclosed as a 
great utopian humane idealist. 

In the ninth year of his long reign in 261 B.c., Asoka adopted 
Buddhism as his state religion at his great.capital of Pataliputra, 
the “Palibothra” of the Greeks, “The City of Sweet-Scented 
Flowers,’ so famous in Indian and Greek legend and story, 
near the modern Patna on the Ganges; and the long lost site 
of which I was privileged to discover, and excavate with the 
recovery of Asoka’s great edict pillar of polished granite and 
numerous other Asokan and later sculptured and inscribed 

PREFACE TO SECOND EDITION. XXi 

remains (see my Discovery of the Lost Palibothra of the Greeks, 
Bengal Secretariat Press, Calcutta, 1892, and The Excauations 
at Pataliputra, idem, Calcutta, 1903). And to set an example 
| to his people, the emperor Asoka himself, becaine in that same 
year a lay disciple, and four years later a Buddhist monk or 
abbot, though unbuddhistically still retaining his vast temporal 
power; but using this latter wholly for the advance of his 
Buddhist church. Thus Asoka is now disclosed as the hitherto 
unrecognised prototype of the later temporal Buddhist popes, 
the Dalai Lamas of Tibet. 

The facts about Asoka’s adoption of Gotama’s Doctrine and 
his transformation of it into a quasi-popular religion, facts 
of such prime importance in the history of the Buddhist 
religion, are fully and authentically recorded by himself in his 
own famous graved edicts on rocks, stonepillars and caves, 
most of which still exist to the present day. 

His adoption of the ethical tenets of Buddha’s “‘Law of 
| Piety’’ as opposed to Buddha’s pessimistic Nirvana theory is 
_ therein ascribed by himself to his deep and heartfelt remorse 
of the horrors of appalling loss of life and sufferings inflicted 
on the people of Kalinga in Southern India by his conquest 
of that province in the ninth year of his reign in 261 B.c. And, 
_ in order to signalize publicly his Buddhistic change of heart, 
he altered his name henceforth and for everafter from “Asoka” 
or “The Sorrowless One” to “The Compassionate, or Humane 
Seeing One,” or literally “The Loving or Kindly Looker 

oe) 

| (apon mankind),” in the Indian vernacular Piye-dasi and in 
| Sanskrit Priya-darsin. On the other hand he habitually calls 
_ himself in his edicts by the title “Beloved of the gods” 
| (Devanam-priya), thus betraying his retention in his form of 
Buddha’s doctrine of an adherence to the old popular Indo- 
Aryan gods. 

In his own words announcing his adoption of Buddha’s Law, 
_ preserved in his still extant Rock Edict XIII., we hear the 
AA 

XXU PREFACE TO SECOND Evo 

moan of a human soul in deep contrition for the loss of human 
life and human suffering which his bloody conquest had 
inflicted on humanity. He says:— 

‘The Beloved of the Gods, The Compassionate King (Piye- dasi) in 
the ninth year of his reign conquered the Kalinga. One hundred and 
fifty thousand persons were thence carried away captive, one hundred 
thousand were there slain, and many times that number perished. 

‘“Tever since the annexation of the Kalingas, The Beloved of the 
God has zealously protected the Law of Piety (Dharma) has been 
devoted to that law and has proclaimed its precepts. 

‘The Beloved of the Gods, feels remorse on account of the con- 
quest of the Kalingas, because during the subjugation of a previously 
unconquered country, slaughter, death and the taking away captive 
of the people necessarily occur, whereat The Beloved of the Gods 
feels profound sorrow and regret. 

“There is another reason for The Beloved of the Gods feeling 
still more regret, namely that in such a country dwell Brahmans 
and ascetics, men of different sects, and households who all practice 
obedience to elders, obedience to father and mother, obedience to 
teachers, proper treatment of friends, acquaintances, comrades, 
relatives, slaves and servants, with fidelity and devotion. To 
such people dwelling in that country happen violence, slaughter, 
and separation from those they love. 

‘Even those persons who are themselves protected retain their 
affections undiminished: thus when ruin falls on their friends, 
acquaintances, comrades, relatives, in this way violence affects 
those who are personally unhurt. All this diffused misery is 
matter for regret to The Beloved of the Gods. For there is no 
country where such communities are not found, including others 
besides Brahmans and ascetics. 

“Though a man should do him an injury The Beloved of the Gods 
holds that it must be patiently borne so far as it can possibly be borne. 

‘Even upon the forest tribes in his dominion The Beloved of 
the Gods has compassion and seeks their conversion, inasmuch as 
the might even of The Beloved of the Gods himself is based on 
repentance. They are warned thus: “Shun evildoing, that ye 
may escape destruction; because The Beloved of the Gods desires 
for all animate beings, security, control over the passion, peace of 
mind and joyousness (happiness). 

“Delight is found in the conquest made by the Law; but The 
Beloved of the Gods thinks nothing of much importance save what 
concerns the next world. 

“The only true conquest is that effected through The Law of 
Piety (Dharma), which avails both for this world and the next. 
Let all the pleasures of my sons be the pleasure of exertion which 
avails both for this world and the next.” 

PREFACE T0 SHCOND EDITION. XXill 

AsoKa’S TRANSFORMATION OF BuDDHA’s BUDDHISM OPTI- 
MISTICALLY AND SEMI-THEIST INTO “‘ PRIMITIVE BUDDHISM.” 

Here it will be especially noted that not only in this edict, 
but in practically all the other numerous edicts by which 
Asoka established Buddhism as a quasi-popular religion, there 
is no mention whatever of Gotama Buddha himself by name 
or title; nor even as the author of The Law of Piety or Dharma, 
which is summarized in para. 4 of above cited edict; and which 
is everywhere hailed by him as the only True Gospel of 
Salvation. 

Indeed, Asoka therein and in nearly all his edicts represents 
“The Law of Piety” or Dharma as having been held from 
immemorial true by Brahmans and others. For in fact, 
Buddha’s ethical code was not original, but was generally 
identical with the ethical code of the Sumerians or Early 
Aryans about three thousand years before Buddha’s day; 
Buddha Gotama having merely grafted it on to his nihilistic 
system in order presumably to popularize the latter. 

Indeed, there were previous Buddhas before Gotama pre- 
sumably teaching the same dogmas. For Asoka erected a 
great inscribed pillar and a mound (which I excavated) over the 
remains of “‘the previous Buddha Konakamana”’ about twelve 
miles from Kapilavastu, the birthplace of Gotama. Thus 
Buddha’s ethical dogma or “The Word,” which was sub- 
stantially the same as that of Christ of Galilee of the Gentiles, 
and also based on brotherly love, was like the latter in that 
“In the beginning was the Word.” . 

Besides, Asoka, the first popularizer of Buddhist ethics as 
a religion, everywhere throughout his edicts significantly 
omits, and obviously consciously represses, all reference to the 
inveterate pessimism of Buddha’s doctrine on the Misery of 
Life, and on Gotama’s desired escape therefrom into the total 
annihilation of Nirvana. On the contrary, Asoka throughout 

XX1V PREFACE TO SECOND EDITION. 

his edicts optimistically praises the Law of Piety as the bringer 
of happiness both in this world and in the next to come, which 
latter he sometimes calls “‘Heaven,’’ and “‘ Bliss.”’ 

Moreover, the old popular Indo-Aryan or Sumerian gods, 
retains such a high position in Asoka’s Buddhism, which 
significantly afterwards became “The Primitive’? Buddhism 
of Ceylon and Burma, that Asoka in promulgating the edicts 
of his “‘Primitive” Buddhism calls himself proudly in nearly 
every stanza “The Beloved of the Gods!” 

Asoka First CoLLects, Forms anD ReEcorps “THE Worp” 
oF BuppHA AS THE FIRST BUDDHIST SCRIPTURES. 

Buddha Gotama, like Christ in later times, wrote down 
nothing himself. His reputed sayings were merely handed 
down orally by his individual votaries, and in many differing 
versions. 

Asoka was the first to collect, compile and record the scattered 
floating oral traditional sayings, tales, and legends of Gotama 
Buddha, and reduce them to writing to form the first Buddhist 
Scriptures as ““The Word ‘of the Buddha; but at a period over 
a century after the death of Gotama. This is analogous 
to the belated and not strictly authentic compilation of the 
Christian Scriptures, which were not reduced to writing till 
some centuries after Christ’s death. 

Also, like the Christian Scriptures and Gospels, Asoka’s 
first records of Buddha’s “‘ Word” soon became vastly expanded 
and overlaid by later priests and prophets on the plea of 
“divine inspiration” or “divine revelation.’’ New verses and 
wholly new chapters were freely added to form the present 
day bulky scriptures of the Ceylon and Burmese “primitive” 
Buddhists; just as with the present day expanded form of the 
Christian Gospels and “‘Word of God,” as now disclosed by 
competent scientific biblical critics, most of whom are “ Doctors 

of Divinity.”’ 

| 

PREFACE TO SECOND EDITION. XXV 

Asoka, in compiling his “Word of The Law,” held several 
Councils of the most learned of his monks at his capital at 
Pataliputra and other chief centres, to sift and try to fix the 
authenticity of the reputed sayings of the Buddha; just as 
later did Constantine and others with reference to their com- 
pilation of the Gospels. Thus the list of the chief books or 
booklets of the Buddhist canon, cited by Asoka in one of his 
later edicts, is infinitesimal compared with the greatly expanded 
canon of the “Primitive” Buddhism of Ceylon and Burma, 
not to speak of Tibet, of which later. 

Asoka EstaBLisHes His DrEvELOPED BuppHIsm as His 
STATE-RELIGION, c. 261 B.c., AND Covers INDIA wiTH BuDD- 
HIST MONUMENTS, MONASTERIES AND TEMPLES. 

Asoka established his developed and transformed Buddhism 
as his State-Religion of his vast empire about 261 B.c. He 
gathered the few scattered Buddhist monks from their her- 
mitages, and housed them in comfortable monasteries. He 

lavished upon his new religion, in establishing and consolidating 
it, the entire colossal wealth and resources of his great empire. 

On this object, he is traditionally reported (and confirmed 
generally by his inscriptions) to have endowed his Buddhist 
Church with “thrice the wealth of the world, which he thrice 
redeemed by his jewels.” 

He covered the land with Buddhist temples and great richly 
endowed Buddhist monasteries with swarming monks. He 
erected countless gigantic monolithic edict pillars of superbly 
polished granite shafts over 70 feet high, crowned with artistic- 
ally carved capitals, at the chief sites visited by Buddha. 
He also carved his edicts on rocks and caves along the chief 
caravan routes, most of which inscribed monuments and 
pillars still exist to the present day. 

To render his form of the Buddhist religion more attractive 
to the lay populace, besides his popular alterations in Buddha’s 

XXV1 PREFACE TO SECOND EDITION. 

doctrine already cited, he erected and endowed beautiful 
shrines, and instituted flower festivals on Buddhist holy days, 
and gaudy popular processions with sacred masked plays. 
In these latter, Buddha is represented as vanquishing the 
demons; and as having the chief Indian gods, Indra, etc., as 
his devotees, disciples and defenders of his faith. In these 
plays the Hindu gods are usually called Nat or ‘“‘ Lord,” from 
the Indian Nath or ‘‘The Lord,’ a common title for the 
Brahmanist god. But in the popular Buddhist displays these 
gods are now made to dance about in undignified antics, as 
if their name was correlated to the Indian Nat or ‘‘dancer,”’ 
an Indian word now well known in its Europeanized form as 
‘““Nautch.”’ 

Asoka also instituted schools for diffusing Buddhist teaching, 
both for monks and laity. And significantly the Indian Budd- 
hist schools retained the secular teaching of the laity in their 
own priestly hands—an experiment not unknown in modern 
Christian hierarchy in our own land at the present day and also 
at public expense. And this priestly hold on such schools 
continued until lately in Burma and Ceylon, and still continues 
in priest-ridden Tibet. 

Moreover, the Buddhist monks rendered themselves indis- 
pensable to the people, by supplying them with spells against 
demons of disease and misfortune, as astrologers casting 
horoscopes, and as family priests officiating at births, marriages, 
sickness and deaths; and as popular physicians. 

And, although richly and unbuddhistically endowing the 
Buddhist monasteries, Asoka nevertheless kept up the fiction 
of the homeless, penniless mendicant monkhood, by arranging 
that the monks were still to beg (some of) their food from door 
to door; for the monasteries were mostly placed on the borders 
of populous towns and cities. And this begging pretence 
still survives in all the later forms of Buddhism, however 
richly endowed, and including Tibet, down to the present day. 

PREFACE TO SECOND EDITION. XXVll 

ASOKA SENDS Myrraps oF MISSIONARIES INTO ADJOINING 
LANDS TO PROPAGATE HIS Farr. 

Asoka sent swarms of missionaries into the adjoining lands 
to propagate his faith, as recorded in his own edicts and 
elsewhere—to Nepal, Ceylon, and Burma, to Kashmir and 
beyond Afghanistan and Bactria to Persia, Mesopotamia and 
Syria-Palestine. He refers repeatedly to missions sent to the 
Seleucid king Antiochus Theos (261-246 B.c.), the grandson 
of Seleucus Nikator who inherited the eastern empire of 
Alexander, including Mesopotamia and Syria, and generally 
called “‘The King of Syria,’ including Assyria and Palestine. 
But there is no historical evidence that any of Asoka’s mission- 
aries reached Tibet. 

Rist oF THEISTIC OR MAHAYANA BUDDHISM IN INDIA IN 
First CENTURY B.C. 

Just before the opening of our Christian era, and seemingly 
as a result of the fall of Asoka’s dynasty depriving the swarms 
of Indian Buddhist monks of their rich state patronage and 
endowments, and leaving them wholly dependent on the 
charity of the laity, we find a new and frankly theistic popular 
form of Buddhism arising. 

This new and fully theistic form of Buddhism, pandering 
to the popular superstitions and craving for a living God and 
hearer and granter of prayers, instead of a long dead saint, 
Buddha, who had admittedly passed completely away into 
total extinction, the Indian Buddhist priests created an ever- 
lasting living Buddha-god in the heavens. They placed him 
in the West “‘to which the Suns all hasten,’ and invested him 
with all the attributes of the old Sun-god of the Sumerians, 
Indo-Aryans and Perso-Aryans. Himself remote from this 
world, he was given beneficent archangels or celestial Bod- 
hisattvas or potential divine Buddhas, whose function it was 

prec or PREFACE TO SECOND EDITION. 

to pass freely between heaven and earth as benefactors of 
men and animals and as suppressers of the earth and air demons. 
And they represented that Gotama Buddha had been merely 
a passing temporary reflex human incarnation of the ever- 
lasting celestial Buddha-god. 

This theistic development of Buddhism was called by its 
Indian Buddhist monkish originators Mahd-yana or “The 
Great Vehicle (for the transportation of the many to salvation),”’ 
as opposed to Buddha’s and even Asoka’s Buddhism, which 
they depreciatingly called Hina-yana or “Little Vehicle (for 
the few), which they alleged was merely a preliminary 
revelation by Buddha and fit only for weaker intellects, until 
“the coming’’ of the more fully-fledged revelation. They 
sophistically reconciled their new developed doctrine with 
that of Buddha and Asoka. And in support of their claims 
they produced, from time to time, new revelations alleged to 
have been communicated to Indian Buddhist sages and prophets 
inspired by the celestial Buddha-god himself. And thus was 
accumulated and formed a much vaster body of revealed 
“Word” and scripture than even the already expanded 
scriptures of the ‘Primitive’ Buddhists of the older 
Hinayana school. 

Regarding this theistic development of Buddhism, it is 
commonly alleged by most European writers on Buddhism, 
basing only on their acquaintance with Hinayana Buddhism 
and what they read into it, that this theistic development was 
not Buddhism at all, because (as they allege) Buddha himself 
was an atheist! Those writers, however, stultify themselves by 
their freely citing Buddha’s own .frequent references to the 
Brahmanist gods as his devotees, attendants and defenders of 
his faith, and also his own alleged former births as Brahmanial 
gods, in the numerous sermons and tales by Buddha in the 
texts of their Hinayana schools which they claim to be true 
Buddhist scriptures. ‘Those texts also contain as we have seen, 

PREFACE TO SECOND EDITION. XX1X 

the developed theistic elements introduced by Asoka in order 
to render Buddhism more popular. 

The newly created everlasting Buddha-god, placed in “The 
Western Paradise” of the Sun, was an idealized solar god and 
named “The Boundless Light” (Amita-bha) and surnamed 
“The Boundless Life” (Amita-yus); but was remote from the 
world of man. He was therefore provided with two archangels 
who directly communicated with mankind as benefactors and 
hearers and answerers of prayers. These two archangels 
were called respectively Ava-lokita or ‘‘The Looking-down 
Lord,” also significantly styled ‘“‘The Saviour,” as he saved 
all those in distress who called upon him. 

And the second was called Tara, the Queen of Heaven and 
the overhead Sky, whose name means “The Saviouress”’ or 
‘“Deliveress’’ or “Goddess of Mercy”’ (as she also believed those 
in distress who called upon her, especially women and children; 
and her chief popular literary prayers along with those to 
Avalokita which had been literally translated from the Indian 
Sanskrit into Tibetan (and current also in China and Japan) 
are now literally rendered into English in the present work. 

Indeed, this Indian Buddhist Tara, ‘“‘Queen of Heaven”’ 
is a glorified form of the old Aryan-Sumerian Asi, the deified 
queen of the first Aryan-Sumerian king Tur, Thor, Dar, or 
In-Dara (“Indra’’) or Jupiter; and in her litanies and art 
representations was clearly the prototype of deified Virgin 
Mary as latterly adopted en bloc by the Romish Church. For, 
her early representations in Indo-Scythic Buddhist art on the 
Greek model with halo, etc., in the Swat Valley and Gandhera 
sculptures of the first century A.D., date many centuries before 
the deification by the Romish Church of Mary as “‘Mother of 
God” and “Goddess of Mercy” on the same model. That 
idea, with her art representations and litanies were presumably 
borrowed from the Buddhists by Romish missionaries to India, 
Bactria, and China after the opening of the Constantine era. 

Aa * 

XXX PREFACE TO SECOND EDITION. 

The name also of Avalokita or “The Looking-down Lord,” 
which Indian name is literally translated into Chinese and 
Japanese as Kwan-yin and Kwan-non for the most popular 
of all Buddhist divinities in those lands, is seen significantly 
to bear a close resemblance to the Buddhistic personal title 
adopted by Asoka, namely “The Compassionate Looker-on 
(Humanity),” and may possibly, I think, have been suggested 
by Asoka’s title. 

Later, during the early centuries of our era, the Indian 
Buddhist priests further developed their idea of the Buddhist 
god-head in the West, by extending the number of gods to 
five, so as geographically to include all directions of the 
universe. Thus in addition to the Buddha-god Amitabha of 
the Western Solar Paradise they created gods for the other 
cardinal directions, East, North and South, and placed the 
fifth in the centre of their universe. But the Buddha-god of 
the West still continued to be the presiding one over this 
world of men and animals—the others were merely “‘ Buddhas 
of Meditation,” yet conferring magical powers on their initiated 
priests, as detailed in these pages. 

PROPAGATION OF THEISTIC OR MAHAYANA BUDDHISM FROM 
Inpia To Cuina, MONGOLIA AND JAPAN, AND TO TIBET. 
How this theistic or Mahayana form of Buddhism was 

propagated from its home in India, fully fiedged to Mongolia 

and China in 61 a.p., and thence to Japan about the sixth 
century A.D., and independently and directly from India to 

Tibet in the seventh century A.D. and onwards to the twelfth 

century A.D., and becoming from the eighth century a.D. the 

the state-religion of Tibet, is fully detailed in this book. 

TIBET PRESERVES UNIQUELY THE Lost SCRIPTURES OF 
INDIAN MAHAYANA BUDDHISM. 

It is also demonstrated in this book that Tibetan Buddhism 
uniquely preserves the great body of the Indian Buddhist 

PREFACE TO SECOND EDITION. XXX1 

Scriptures, Canon and Commentaries of the Mahayana school 
of Indian Buddhism—i.e. for the period when Buddhism was 
for the first time a truly popular religion in India—which 
scriptures were totally destroyed in India by the fanatical 
barbarian Mohammadan invaders in the twelfth century 4.D.; 
and of which only relatively few books had been preserved 
in Nepal, Mongolia, and China. And the remarkably literal 
and scholarly translations of those Indian scriptures into 
Tibetan, effected by the most learned and scholarly Indian 
Buddhist monks of the day, has been attested by comparison 
with the Sanskrit original texts of the few books of which 
copies have survived. 

_ APOTHEOSIS OF BUDDHISM IN TIBET. 

In Tibet, theistic Indian Buddhism has reached its acme in 
the apotheosis of Buddha himself and in the adoration of his 
heirarch as a divinity during his lifetime. This has happened 
since the assumption there in 1251 a.p. of divine honours by the 
Lamaist Popes on their acquisition of the temporal sway of that 
land, gifted to them by the famous Mongol emperor of China, 
Kublai Khan, and thus making these popes analogous to Asoka 
of old in India in Indian Buddhism. Though Asoka himself 
nowhere claimed divine honours other than in his cherished title 
“Beloved of the Gods,” he claimed “‘the divine right to rule”’ of 
Aryan Kings, a logical outcome of the old Aryan worship of 
ancestors, in popular gratitude for their having brought to the 
people and enhanced the blessings of Civilization, which price- 
less boon, as I have conclusively demonstrated, was conferred 
upon the world by the early Aryan-Sumerian kings. 

The formal attribution of divine donours awarded:to deceased 
Roman emperors, latterly degenerated by anticipation into 
adoration of the living Roman emperors of the Holy Roman 
Empire; and is now reflected more or less on the Romish Popes 

XXXIl PREFACE TO SECOND EDITION. 

with their infallibility. There seems, however, no reason to 
suppose that this Roman precedent had anything to do with 
the assumption of divine honours by the Lamaist Popes, but 
merely the boundless ambitions of an autocratic priestcraft. 
On the other hand, it is noteworthy that the Buddhist god of 
whom the Dalai Lamas claim to be the human incarnation is 
the most popular of all the Buddhist gods, namely Avalokita, 
“The Looking-down Compassionate Lord and Saviour of Men,” 
whose name as we have seen was presumably coined on the 
Buddhistic title adopted by the first Buddhist Pope with 
temporal sway, the great Aryan Indian emperor, Asoka, the 
Constantine of Buddhism. 

THE Late GRAND oR Davat LAMA, THE TIBETAN POPE. 

The title “Dalai” or “Ocean or Universal Lama” is a 
Mongol title conferred on the Grand Lama of Tibet by the 
Mongol emperor Kublai Khan in the thirteenth century 4.D., 
along with the temporal sovereignty of Tibet in return for that 
emperor being officially anointed by that hierarch as Buddhist 
emperor of China and making Lamaism the state religion of the 
Mongols. 

The last reigning Dalai Lama, fully described in this book 
and in my Lhasa and Its Mysteries has just suddenly died of 
‘‘heart-failure’’ on the 17th December, 1933; and his successor 
has not yet been “discovered.” For, according to the Lamaist 
fiction of his perpetual rebirth as Dalai Lama, the census 
of all the male Tibetan infants born on the day of his death or 
immediately thereafter has not yet been completed by the 
great rival monasteries, each eager to secure an infant successor 
from their own neighbourhood in order to wield the temporal 
rule of Tibet in their own self-interest during the infants’ long 
minority. So greatly was this temporal rule sought after by 
the intriguing lamas, that for a long period no infant Grand 

PREFACE TO SECOND EDITION. XXX1ll 

Lama was allowed to outlive his minority. But the late Dalai 
Lama was allowed to become an exception. 

The son of a poor and illiterate peasant, this Lamaist pope 
proved himself on reaching his majority to be a fairly just 
temporal ruler of Tibet, according to his limited oriental lights 
and Lamaist superstitions. On his precipitate flight from his 
Lhasa capital before its capture by wa British “Mission” in 
1904, he spent some years amongst his faithful adoring Mongols 
in Mongolia; but eventually returned to his temporal and 
spiritual rule at Lhasa on the invitation of gw-British Indian 
Government; and even visited on a pilgrimage the chief old 
Buddhist sites in the Buddhist Holy Land in India, including 
Buddha’s birthplace discovered by me, as the honoured guest of 

SE Indian ‘Government. 

Hostitity oF LAMAS TO WESTERN CIVILIZATION AND 
WESTERN EDUCATION. 

In order to assist the Tibetans towards acquiring some 
western civilization, this Dalai Lama was induced by -eti 
Indian Government to send them the young sons of Tibetan 
lay landowners, merchants and nobles to receive western 
education on civilized lines. Those boys were sent to India 
_ from time to time in batches, and were thence sent on to the 
great public schools in England, at Harrow and Rugby, and one 
to Sandhurst for military training. But it all proved a miserable 
and total failure, through the inveterate hostility of the 
all-powerful and superstitious intriguing Lamas of the great 
monasteries in fear of losing their firm hold on the ignorant 
populace; and more especially so, as Tibet by this time had 
become again isolated and independent, and freed from the 
suzerainty of China, by the withdrawal of Chinese troops to 
defend their own capitals against Soviet Russia and Japanese 
aggression; and Tibet was again solely under Lamaist priestly 

domination. 

XXXIV PREFACE TO SECOND EDITION. 

Thus, those English educated Tibetan youths on their return 
to Tibet “completely westernized,” and from whose western 
education such great things were hoped for, they were relegated 
by the Lama rulers to obscurity and given no official position of 
any consequence. Only the one specialist in electrical engineer- 
ing was retained by the Dalai Lama to light electrically his 
palace, and look after his English motor car! Similarly, the 
industrial school set up at Gyantse in Central Tibet on the 
trade route from India to Lhasa and Mongolia in 1923 under 
English headmasters at the request of the Dalai Lama for the 
western education of the sons of Tibetan lay officials and 
wealthy landowners and merchants who did not wish their sons 
to leave Tibet, was finally closed down after only three years 
through the hostile Lamas fearing that the western teaching 
would undermine their own strangle-hold on the minds of the 
rising generation. Such is the pitiable decadent effect of 
uncurbed priestly rule in the most priest-ridden land in the 
world. 

My PERSONAL CORRESPONDENCE WITH THE DALaI LAMA. 

The late Dalai Lamaist Pope was from all accounts an 
amiable and easily led (or misled) man. I had not the fortune 
personally to meet His Holiness at Lhasa in 1904, as I had 
hoped, because he had timidly fled from Lhasa the night before 
the capture of his capital by our British “‘ Mission”’; but I enjoyed 
the intimacy of his Regent, a most amiable and venerable old 
man, as described with his photograph in my Lhasa and Its 
Mysteries. Moreover, by the time the Dalai Lama had 
returned from Mongolia and visited India as the guest of -emae 
Indian Government I had retired from India. 

As however, my old assistant, the Tibetan scholar Mr. David 
Macdonald, whom I had taken on my staff to Lhasa in 1904, 
and who had soon thereafter been appointed British Trade 
Agent in Tibet, and residing largely in Lhasa, had become an 

PREFACE TO SECOND EDITION. XXXV 
intimate personal friend of the Dalai Lama, informed me that 
the latter was greatly interested in the illustrations in bcth my 
Buddhism of Tibet and Lhasa and Its Mysteries; and that the 
Dalai Lama would certainly write to me if I wrote to him. 
This I postponed doing until last autumn, and forwarded my 
letter through Mr. Macdonald, who has now retired from the 
service and resides on the Tibetian frontier beyond Darjeeling. 
In my letter, I referred to my lifelong study of Buddhism at 
first-hand in the different lands of its adoption, and to my 
following the footsteps of Gotama Buddha in the Buddhist 
Holy Land in India having led to the discovery, not only of 
his long lost birthplace, but also to several of his lost hermitages 
with inscriptual remains, including a vase of his bodily relics, 
all of which established the historicity of the Buddha beyond 
all further doubt. 

Now it is interesting and noteworthy that one of the last 
letters written by the Dalai Lama, if not as Mr. Macdonald 
believes the very last of all letters written by him in his last 
illness, was written to his friend Macdonald and contained a 
special message from His Holiness to me. It was written on 
the 7th of December last, ten days before his death on the 17th 
December. 

His letter, written in Tibetan says :— 

“The reason for writing ...... 

“The letter from the retired British Government doctor, 
Colonel Waddell has been duly received. As the subject is so 
very great and important, J shall send a special reply to him 
without delay. This letter is sent with a silken scarf of 
ceremonial greeting on the auspicious date of the 20th day of 
the 10th Tibetan month of The Water-Bird year (= 7th day of 
December, 1933).” Signed by the hand of the Dalai Lama with 
the Great Red Seal of the mystic magical Indian spell Om. 

L. A. WADDELL. 
22nd January, 1934. 

PREFACE TO FIRST EDITION. 

N° apology is needed for the production at the pre- 

sent time of a work on the Buddhism of Tibet, 
or * Laimaism” as it has been called, after its priests. 
Notwithstanding the increased attention which in recent 
years has been directed to Buddhism by the speculations 
of Schopenhauer and Hartmann, and the widely felt 
desire for fuller information as to the conditions and 
sources of Eastern religion, there exists no Huropean 
book giving much insight into the jealously guarded 
religion of Tibet, where Buddbism wreathed in romance 
has now its chief stronghold. 

The only treatise on the subject in English, is Emil 
Schlagintweit's Buddhism in Tibet! published over thirty 
years ago, and now out of print. A work which, however 
admirable with respect to the time of its appearance, was 
admittedly fragmentary, as its author had never been in 
contact with ‘Tibetans. And the only other European 
book on Lamaism, excepting Giorgi’s curious compilation 
of last century, is Koppen’s Die Lamaische LHierarchie 

' Leipzig and London, 1863. That there is no lack of miscellaneous litera- 

ture on Tibet and Limaism may be seen from the bibliographical lst in the 
appendix ; but it is all of a fragmentary and often conflicting character. 

PREFACE. XXXVi 

und Kirche ' published thirty-five years ago,and also a com- 
pilation and out of print. Since the publication of these 
two works much new information has been gained, though 
scattered through more or less inaccessible Russian, 
German, French, and Asiatic journals. And this, com- 
bined with the existing opportunities for a closer study of 
Tibet and its customs, renders a fuller and more syste- 
matic work now possible. 

Some reference seems needed to my special facilities for 
undertaking this task. In addition to having personally 
studied “southern Buddhism” in Burma and Ceylon; and 
“northern Buddhism” in Sikhim, Bhotan and Japan; 
and exploring Indian Buddhism in its remains in “the 
Buddhist Holy Land,” and the ethnology of ‘Tibet and its 
border tribes in Sikhim, Asam, and upper Burma; and 
being one of the few Europeans who have entered the 
territory of the Grand Lama, I have spent several years in 
studying the actualities of Lamaism as explained by its 
priests, at points much nearer Lhasa than any utilized for 
such a purpose, and where I could feel the pulse of the 
sacred city itself beating in the large communities of its 
natives, many of whom had left Lhasa only ten or twelve 
days previously. 

On commencing my enquiry I found it necessary to 
learn the language, which is peculiarly difficult, and known 
to very few Europeans. And afterwards, realizing the 
rigid secrecy maintained by the Lamas in regard to their 
seemingly :chaotic rites and symbolism, | felt compelled to 
purchase a Lamaist temple with its fittings ; and prevailed 
on the officiating priests to explain to me in full detail 
the symbolism and the rites as they proceeded. - Perceiv- 
ing how much I was interested, the Lamas were so oblig- 

! Berlin, 1859. 

XXXViil PREFACE. 

ing as to interpret in my favour a prophetic account 
which exists in their scriptures regarding a Buddhist in- 
carnation in the West. They convinced themselves that 
I was a reflex of the Western Buddha, Amitabha, and 
thus they overcame their conscientious scruples, and im- 
parted information freely. With the knowledge thus 
gained, I visited other temples and monasteries critically, 
amplifying my information, and engaging a small staff of 
Lamas in the work of copying manuscripts, and searching 
for texts bearing upon my researcnes. Enjoying in these 
ways special facilities for penetrating the reserve of 
‘Tibetan ritual, and obtaining direct from Lhasa and 
Tashi-lhunpo most of the objects and explanatory 
material needed, I have elicited much information on 
Lamaist theory and practice which is altogether new. 

The present work, while embodying much original 
research, brings to a focus most of the information on 
Lamaism scattered through former publications. And 
bearing in mind the increasing number of general readers 
interested in old world ethics, custom and myth, and in the 
ceaseless effort of the human heart in its insatiable craving 
for absolute truth; as well as the more serious students of 
Lamaism amongst orientalists, travellers, missionaries and 
others, 1 have endeavoured to give a clear insight into 
the structure, prominent features and cults of this system, 
and have relegated to smaller type and footnotes the more 
technical details and references required by specialists. 

The special characteristics of the book are its detailed 
accounts of the external facts and curious symbolism of 
Buddhism, and its analyses of the internal movements 
leading to Lamaism and its sects and cults. It provides 
material culled from hoary Tibetan tradition and explained 
to me by Lamas for elucidating many obscure points in 
primitive Indian Buddhism and its later symbolism. Thus 

PREFACE. XXX1X 

a clue is supplied to several disputed doctrinal points of 
fundamental importance, as for example the formula of 
the Causal Nexus. And it interprets much of the inter- 
esting Mahayana and Tantrik developments in the later 
Indian Buddhism of Magadha. 

It attempts to disentangle the early history of Lamaism 
from the chaotic growth of fable which has invested it. 
With this view the nebulous Tibetan “ history ” so-called 
of the earlier periods has been somewhat critically 
examined in the light afforded by some scholarly Lamas 
‘and contemporary history; and all fictitious chronicles, 
such as the Mani-kah-’bum, hitherto treated usually as 
historical, are rejected as authoritative for events which 
happened a thousand years before they were written and 
for a time when writing was admittedly unknown in 
Tibet. If, after rejecting these manifestly fictitious 
“histories” and whatever is supernatural, the residue 
cannot be accepted as altogether trustworthy history, it 
at least affords a fairly probable historical basis, which 
seems consistent and in harmony with known facts and 
unwritten tradition. 

It will be seen that I consider the founder of Lama- 
ism to be Padma-sambhava—a person to whom previous 
writers are wont to refer in too incidental a manner. 
Indeed, some careful writers! omit all mention of his 
name, although he is considered by the Lamas of all sects 
to be the founder of their order, and by the majority of 
them to be greater and more deserving of worship than 
Buddha himself. 

Most of the chief internal: movements of Lamaism are 
now for the first time presented in an intelligible and 
systematic form. Thus, for example, my account of its 

1 E.g, W. R. 8. Ralston in his Libeton Tales. 

xl PREFACE. 
sects may be compared with that given by Schlagintweit,' 
to which nothing practically had been added.” 

As Lamaism lives mainly by the senses and spends its 
strength in sacerdotal functions, it is particularly rich in 
ritual. Special prominence, therefore, has been given to 

its ceremonial, all the more so as ritual preserves many 
interesting vestiges of archaic times. My special facilities 
for acquiring such information has enabled me to supply 
details of the principal rites, mystic and other, most of 
which were previously undescribed. Many of these 
exhibit in combination ancient Indian and pre-Buddhist 
Tibetan cults. The higher ritual, as already known, 
invites comparison with much in the Roman Church ; 
and the fuller details now afforded facilitate this com- 
parison and contrast. 

But the bulk of the Lamaist cults comprise much 
deep-rooted devil-worship and sorcery, which I describe 
with some fulness. For Lamaism is only thinly and im- 
perfectly varnished over with Buddhist symbolism, beneath 
which the sinister growth of poly-demonist superstition 
darkly appears. 

The religious plays and festivals are also described. 
And a chapter is added on popular and domestic Lama- 
ism to show the actual working of the religion in every- 
day life as a system of ethical belief and practice. 

The advantages of the very numerous illustrations— 
about two hundred in number, mostly from originals 
brought from Lhasa, and from photographs by the author 
—must be obvious.” Mr. Rockhill and Mr. Knight have 
kindly permitted the use of a few of their illustrations. 

SO nal sel 2 * But see note on p. 69.

original objects ; and some have been taken from Giorgi, Huc, Pander, and others. 

PREFACE. xli 

A full index has been provided, also a chronological 
table and bibliography. 

I have to acknowledge the special aid afforded me by 
the learned Tibetan Lama, Padma Chho Phél; by that 
venerable scholar-the Mongolian Lama She-rab Gya-ts’6 ; 
by the Nin-ma Lama, Ur-gyin Gya-ts’6, head of the 
Yang-gang monastery of Sikhim and a noted explorer of 
Tibet; by Tun-yig Wang-dan and Mr. Dor-je Ts’e-ring ; 
by S’ad-sgra S’ab-pe, one of the Tibetan governors of 
Lhasa, who supplied some useful information, and a few 
manuscripts; and by Mr. A.W. Paul, C.I.E., when pursuing 
my researches in Sikhim. 

And I am deeply indebted to the kind courtesy of 
Professor C. Bendall for much special assistance and 
advice; and also generally to my friend Dr. Islay 
Muirhead. 

Of previous writers to whose books I am specially 
under obligation, foremost must be mentioned Csoma 
Korosi, the enthusiastic Hungarian scholar and pioneer 
of ‘Tibetan studies, who first rendered the Lamaist stores 
of information accessible to Europeans.! Though to 
Brian Houghton Hodgson, the father of modern critical 
study of Buddhist doctrine, belongs the credit of dis- 
covering” the Indian nature of the bulk of the Lamaist 
literature and of procuring the material for the detailed 
analyses by Csoma and Burnouf. My indebtedness to 
Koppen and Schlagintweit has already been mentioned. 

1 Alexander Csoma of Korés, in the Transylvanian circle of Hungary, like 
most of the subsequent writers on Lamaism, studied that system in Ladak. 
After publishing his Dictionary, Grammar, and Analysis, he proceeded to 
Darjiling in the hope of penetrating thence to Tibet, but died at Darjiling on 
the llth April, 1842, a few days after arrival there, where his tomb now bears 
a suitable monument, erected by the Government of India. For details of his 
life and labours, see his biography by Dr. Duka. 

2 Asiatic Researches, xvi., 1828. 

xln PREFACE. 

Jaeschke’s great dictionary is a mine of information on 
technical and doctrinal definitions. The works of Giorgi, 
Vasiliev, Schiefner, Foucaux, Rockhill, Kitel, and Pander, 
have also proved most helpful. The Narrative of Travels 
in Tibet by Babu Saratcandra Das, and his translations 
from the vernacular literature, have afforded some use- 
ful details. The Indian Survey reports and Markham’s 
Tibet have been of service; and the systematic treatises 
of Professors Rhys Davids, Oldenberg and Beal have 
supplied several. useful indications. 

The vastness of this many-sided subject, far beyond the 
scope of individual experience, the backward state of 
our knowledge on many points, the peculiar difficulties 
that beset the research, and the conditions under which 
the greater part of the book was written—in the scant 
leisure of a busy official life—these considerations may, I 
trust, excuse the frequent crudeness of treatment, as well 
as any errors which may be present, for I cannot fail to 
have missed the meaning occasionally, though sparing 
no pains to ensure accuracy. But, if my book, not- 
withstanding its shortcomings, proves of real use to 
those seeking information on the Buddhism of Tibet, 
as well as on the later Indian developments of Buddhism, 
and to future workers in these fields, I shall feel amply 
rewarded for all my labours. 

L. AvustinE WADDELL. 
London, 
31st October, 1894.

PRONUNCIATION. 

The general reader should remember as a rough rule that in thie 
oriental names the vowels are pronounced as in German, and the con- 
sonants as in English, except ¢ which is pronounced as ‘ ch,” 7 as “ng” 
and was “ny.” In particular, words like Buddha are pronounced as if 
spelt in English ‘“ Bédd-dha,” Sakya Muni as “ Shi-kya Méé-nee,” and 
Karma as “ Kur-ma.” 

The spelling of Tibetan names is peculiarly uncouth and startling to 
the English reader. Indeed, many of the names as transcribed from 
the vernacular seem unpronounceable, and the difficulty is not diminished 
by the spoken form often differing widely from the written, owing chiefly 
to consonants having changed their sound or dropped out of speech 
altogether, the so-called ‘“ silent consonants.” ' Thus the Tibetan word 
for the border-country which we, following the Nepalese, call Sikhim is 
spelt ’bras-ljons, and pronounced “ Dén-jong,” and bkra-s’ts is “ Ta-shi.” 
When, however, I have found it necessary to give the full form of these 
names, especially the more important words translated from the Sans- 
krit, in order to recover their original Indian form and meaning, I have 
referred them as far as possible to footnotes, 

The transcription of the Tibetan letters follows the system adopted bv 
Jaeschke in his Dictionary, with the exceptions noted below,’ and cor- 
responds closely with the analogous system for Sanskritic words given 
over the page. The Tibetan pronunciation is spelt phonetically in the 
dialect of Lhasa. 

1 Somewhat analogous to the French ds parlent. 

2 The exceptions mainly are those requiring very specialized diacr itical 
marks, the letters which are there (JAESCHKE’S Dict., p. viii.), pronounced ga 
as a prefix, cha, nya, the ha in several forms as the basis for vowels ; these I 
have rendered by g, ch’, iand’ respectively. Inseveral cases I have spelt words 
according to Csoma’s system, by which the silent consonants are italicized. 

l PRONUNCIATION. 

For the use of readers who are conversant with the Indian alphabets, 
and the system popularly known in India as “the Hunterian,” the 
following table, in the order in which the sounds are physiologically 
produced—an order also followed by the Tibetans—will show the 
system of spelling Sanskritic words, which is here-adopted, and which 
it will be observed, is almost identical with that of the widely used 
dictionaries of Monier-Williams and Childers. The different forms 
used in the Tibetan for aspirates and palato-sibilants are placed within 
brackets :— 

(gutturals) k kh(k’) g gh n 
(palatals) c(c’) ch(ch’) j jh ii 
(cerebrals) t th d dh n 
(dentals) t th(t’) d dh n 
(labials) p ph(p’) b bh m 
(palato-sibil.) (ts) (ts’) (2 &ds) (2)

INTRODUCTORY. 

IBET, the mystic 
Land of the Grand 
Lama, joint God 
and King of many mil- 
lions, is still the most 
impenetrable country in 
the world. Behind its 
icy barriers, reared round it by 
Nature herself, and almost un- 
surmountable, its priests guard 
its _passes jealously against 
foreigners. 
, Few Europeans have: ever 

entered Tibet; and none for half a century have reached the 
B 

vy INTRODUCTORY. 

sacred city. Of the travellers of later times who have dared to 
enter this dark land, after scaling its frontiers and piercing 

Virw 1nTO 8.W. 'TrnEt 
(from Tang-kar La Pass, 16,600 ft.). 

its passes, and thrusting themselves into its snow-swept deserts, 
even the most intrepid have failed to penetrate farther than the 
outskirts of its central province.t And the information, thus 
perilously gained, has, with the ae of Mr. Rocktiil’s been 

1 ‘The few Europeans who have penetrated Central Tibet have mostly been Roman 
missionaries. The first European to reach Lhasa seems to have been Friar Odoric, of 
Pordenne, about 1380 A.p, o1 his return from Cathay (Col. Yuix’s Cuthay and the Road 
Thither, i., 149, and C. MarkHam’s Tcbet, xlvi.). The capital city of Tibet referred to 
by him with its “.tdasx:” or Pope is believed to have been Lhasa. In 1661 the Jesuits 
Albert Dorville and Johanu Gruher visited Lhasa on their way from China to India. 
In 1706 the Capuchine fathers Josepho de Asculi and Francisco Marie de Toun pene- 
trated to Lhasa from Bengal. In1716 the Jesuit Desideri reached it from Kashinir and 
Ladak. In 1741 a Capuchine mission under Horacio de la Penna also succeeded in 
getting there, and the large amount of information collected by them supplied Father 
A. Giorgi with the material for his Alphabetum Tibetanum, published at Rome in 1762. 
The friendly reception accorded this party created hopes of Lhasa becoming a centre 
for Roman missionaries; and a Vicar cpostolicus for Lhasa is still nominated and 
appears in the “ Aneuario portifcco,” though of course he cannot reside witlun Tibet. 
In 1811 Lhasa was reached by Manning, a friend of Charles Lamb, and the only English- 
man who seems ever to have got there; for most authorities are agreed that Moor- 
croft, despite the story told to M. Huc, never reached it, But, Manning unfortunately 
left only a whimsical diary, scarcely even descriptive of his fascinating adventures. 
The subsequent, and the last, Europeans to reach Lhasa were the Lazarist mission- 
aries, Huc and Gabet, in 1845. Huc’s entertaining account of his journey is well 
known. He was soon expelled, aud since then China has aided Tibet in opposing 
foreign ingress by strengthening its political and military barriers, as recent ex- 
plorers: Pr cjivalsky, Rockhill, Bonvalot, Bower, Miss Taylor, ctc., have found to their 
cost ; though some are sanguine that the Sikhim Trade Convention of this year (1894) 
1s probably Ale thin edge of the wedge to open up the country, and that at no distant 
date Tibet will be prevailed on to relax its jealous exclusiveness, so that, ’ere 1900, 
even Cook’s tourists nay visit the Lamaist Vatican. 

PUNO Pik SUPERNATURAL, 3 

a 
almost entirely geographical, leaving the customs of this forbidden 
land still a field for fiction and romance. 

Thus we are told that, amidst the solitudes of this “Land of the 
Supernatural” repose the spirits of “The Masters,” the JM/ ahdtmas, 

» 

CAPTAIN OF GUARD OF Donu-xya Pass. 
(8.-Western Tibet.) 

whose astral bodies slumber in unbroken peace, save when they 
condescend to work some petty miracle in the world below. 

In presenting here the actualities of the cults and customs of 
Tibet; and lifting higher than before the veil which still hides its 

r INTRODUCTORY. 

mysteries from European eyes, the subject may be viewed under 
the following sections :— 

a. Historical. The changes in primitive Buddhism leading to 
Limaism, and the origins of Limaism and its sects. 

b. DocrrinaL. The metaphysical sources of the doctrine. The 
doctrine and its morality and literature. 

c. Monastic. ‘The Limaist order. Its curriculum, daily life, 
dress, etc., discipline, hierarchy and incarnate-deities and re- 
embodied saints. 

d. BuILDINGS. Monasteries, temples, monuments, and shrines. 

e. PANTHEON AND MyrHoLocy, including saints, images, 
fetishes, and other sacred objects and symbols. 

f. RITUAL AND SORCERY, comprising sacerdotal services for the 
laity, astrology, oracles and divination, charms and necromancy. 

g. FESTIVALS AND SACRED PLays, with the mystic plays and 
masquerades. 

h. PorpuLaR AND Domestic LamaIsm in every-day life, customs, 
and folk-lore. 

Such an exposition will afford us a fairly full and complete 
survey of one of the most active, and least known, forms of exist- 
ing Buddhism; and will present incidentally numerous other 
topics of wide and varied human interest. 

For Limaism is, indeed, a microcosm of the growth of religion 
and myth among primitive people; and in large degree an object- 
lesson of their advance from barbarism towards civilization. And 
it preserves for us much of the old-world lore and petrified beliefs 
of our Aryan ancestors.

BUDDHA'S LIFE AND DEAT, 

ba | 

his reappearance, confident that he had discovered the secrets of 
deliverance; his carrying the good tidings of the truth from town 
to town ; his effective protest against the cruel sacrifices of the 
Brahmans, and his relief of much of the suffering inflicted upon 
helpless animals and often human beings, in the name of religion ; 
his death, full of years and honours, and the subsequent 

VATA: 

Buppua’s DEATH 
(from a Tibetan picture, after Griinwedel), 

burial of his relics,—all these episodes in Buddha’s life are familiar 
to English readers in the pages of Sir Edwin Arnold’s Light of 
Asia, and other works. 

His system, which arose as a revolt against the one-sided de- 
velopment of contemporary religion and ethics, the caste-debase- 
ment of man and the materializing of God, took the form, as 
we shall see, of an agnostic idealism, which threw away ritual 
and sacerdotalism altogether. 

Its tolerant creed of universal benevolence, quickened by the 
bright example of a pure and noble life, appealed to the feelings 

8 CHANGES IN PRIMITIVE BUDDIFSM., 

of the people with irresistible force and directness, and soon 
gained for the new religion many converts in the Ganges Valley. 

And it gradually gathered a brotherhood of monks, which after 
Buddha’s death became subject to a succession of “ Patriarchs,”’ 
who, however, possessed little or no centralized hierarchal power, 
nor, had at least the earlier of them, any fixed abode. 

About 250 B.c. it was vigorously propagated by the great 
Emperor Asoka, the Constantine of Buddhism, who, adopting it 
as his State-religion, zealously spread it throughout his own vast 
empire, and sent: many missionaries into the adjoining lands to 
diffuse the faith. Thus was it transported to Burma,? Siam, 
Ceylon, and other islands on the south, to Nepal® and the countries 
to the north of India, Kashmir, Bactria, Afghanistan, etc. 

In 61 a.p. it spread to China,’ and through China, to Corea, and,
